parts.text:
To help you draft the perfect message, I have categorized these by the "vibe" or context of your situation. Choose the one that best fits your relationship with them.

### Option 1: Professional & Direct (Best for a boss or colleague)
"Hi [Name], I’m checking in to see if you’ve had a chance to look over [Project/Document Name]. Please let me know if you need any additional information from my side to help move this forward. Thanks!"

### Option 2: Casual & Friendly (Best for a teammate or peer)
"Hey [Name]! Just bubbling this to the top of your inbox. Let me know when you have a moment to take a look at [Project/Task] so we can keep things moving. Thanks!"

### Option 3: Deadline-Driven (Best if time is running out)
"Hi [Name], just a friendly follow-up on [Task]. We are aiming to wrap this up by [Date/Time], so please let me know if there’s anything I can do to help facilitate the review on your end!"

### Option 4: The "Help Me Help You" Approach (Best if you’re waiting on them)
"Hi [Name], I’m currently waiting on [Task/Decision] to proceed with the next steps for [Project]. Do you have an estimate of when you might be able to get to this? I want to make sure I’m hitting our target dates."

### Option 5: Ultra-Short (Best for Slack, Teams, or Text)
"Hi [Name]! Following up on [Task]—let me know if you’ve had a chance to review. Thanks!"

---

### Tips for a better follow-up:
* **Reply in the same thread:** Always reply to your previous email so the person can see the context without searching for it.
* **Check your timing:** Wait at least 2–3 business days before following up (unless it’s an urgent deadline).
* **Offer an out:** Sometimes people are just busy. Adding "No rush, just wanted to keep it on your radar" can make you sound less demanding while still getting the job done.
* **Make it easy:** If you are asking for a review, link the document directly in the follow-up message so they don't have to hunt for it.
